comments.title: Speaking of births and monsters
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

This is a bit embarrassing, but might I ask about natural magic charms for a newborn. 🫢

And a lore question: While staying at a hospital I observed/overheard that a couple received a baby 6 weeks to early and en caul ( in the skin ). They think it is supposedly a sign the baby is a luck bringer. This might not be as simple as that. Might I ask about lore related to such children.

Best regards,
V

replies.title: Re: Speaking of births and monsters
replies.content:
For a newborn, get a piece of red coral and hang it above the infant's bed, out of reach. That's an ancient charm that brings good fortune.

In American folklore, a baby born with a caul is destined to have clairvoyance and other psychic gifts.

replies.title: Re: Speaking of births and monsters
replies.content:
1.) thank you.

2.) Interesting. The couple has mixed Thai roots. I myself, while doing research for a previous MM, reread some articles about folk traditions in Southern Europe that mention it. Seems to be a common pattern of belief.

replies.title: Re: Speaking of births and monsters
replies.content:
My grandmother had a story about a doctor who carefully dried and folded a caul so he could keep it in the compartment at the back of his pocket watch.
Forgot about that until now.
What would that have done for him?

replies.title: Re: Speaking of births and monsters
replies.content: I have no idea. That's one I've never heard of.

replies.title: Re: Speaking of births and monsters
replies.content: Traditionally, at least in Britain/Early America, the dried cauls were said to prevent drowning, especially at sea. They were sought after by sailors in the 1600s who paid large sums for them.

replies.title: Re: Speaking of births and monsters
replies.content: Southern Slavic sources from Trieste speak of the exactly the same tradition.

replies.title: Re: Speaking of births and monsters
replies.content:
Interesting. My youngest son was still inside the caul when he "coronated" (i don't know the term in english, but in spanish this is how we call the moment when the new-born head can be seen.) and for about half-way outside, although the doctor broke it at some point, because she was afraid it can make labor more dificult. I remember that she comented about it being rare, and having something to do with a sign of magic and luck.

Do you think that the fact that she break it diminished the efect?

Guillem

replies.title: Re: Speaking of births and monsters
replies.content: The English word for that is "crowned," because it's the crown of the head that shows first in a normal birth. No, cauls often get torn or broken in the birth process; I've never heard that this diminished their power any.

replies.title: Re: Speaking of births and monsters
replies.content: I've heard of Greek ladies hanging hot peppers around the cradle to ward off bad things resulting from envy. In context: it was specifically a response to people complimenting the baby!

replies.title: Re: Speaking of births and monsters
replies.content:
I had read that the caul was believed to give the owner protection from drowning. In some areas those born with the caul would sell it to sailors for that purpose. I think this applied to being born actually in the unbroken caul--which is less common these days since rupturing the sac is one way to speed labor and is somewhat routine in Western medicine.

Rita

comments.title: The Apocalyptic Impulse
comments.content: Hi JMG, do you have any thoughts on the impulses some people seem to have to fulfill prophecies of doom? A classic example is people who read science fiction stories about a horrifying possible future (blanket surveillance, killer robots, etc.) and then work to create that future even as they acknowledge it's not desirable. It's shown up in fields of politics and business along with technology. How could the percolation of these ideas in the collective unconscious have this siren-like effect on people? It reminds me a bit of the lure of the infernal, the interest that some people mention in contacting evil powers even as they know this is a terrible idea.

replies.title: Re: The Apocalyptic Impulse
replies.content: It's a tangled mess. Some people want to create such a future because they hate their lives and would rather have anything else; some people do it because they hate other people and want to punish them. Then there's the lure of the infernal, which is very much an issue here; especially in an era of mandatory niceness, some people long to break out of a stifling social framework, and evil is the only way they can imagine doing it.

replies.title: Re: The Apocalyptic Impulse
replies.content:
Isn't there a simpler principle that would already explain the observed anomalies there? Like, whatever the thing is that gives rise to "what you contemplate, you imitate", or "focus on what you want to see more of", or that makes it important to phrase affirmations using words whose evocativeness directly builds up a description of the desired outcome (rather than depending on the meaning getting built up to include negations in a properly hooked up way, despite how the negations turn out to be evocativity-neutral).

It makes me wonder if writing Nineteen Eighty-four to be so (at the time) viscerally impactful was a good strategic call, or it was almost a good call at the time but would have needed some follow-through later that it wasn't going to get, or what. Now that I think of it, it also makes me wonder about the in-universe encyclopedia-like overview retrospective article about Newspeak, that Orwell put after the end of the main narrative. Maybe that would have blunted the potential adverse magical effect somehow, by making it canon that the Ingsoc project failed?

replies.title: Re: The Apocalyptic Impulse
replies.content: Doubtless that's also a factor. The thing that made 1984 at once such a powerful novel and such an inaccurate prediction is that it wasn't a prediction -- it was a satire, a brilliant one, of what was actually going on in Britain specifically and the Western industrial nations generally when Orwell wrote. Everything in it was simply a rhetorical exaggeration of business as usual. By putting that retrospective at the end, Orwell was saying that the existing order of society was doomed to failure too. He was a very capable writer!

replies.title: Re: The Apocalyptic Impulse
replies.content: RE: 1984 - I'm sure there was lots of inspiration around for the author. Joseph Goebbels especially comes to mind.

replies.title: Re: The Apocalyptic Impulse
replies.content: Ah, but if I recall correctly, Orwell himself explained that most of the inspiration came from Britain. Newspeak, for example, was inspired by a project to create a dumbed-down "Basic English" for the colonies.

replies.title: Re: The Apocalyptic Impulse
replies.content:
I could be wrong, but I thought the book partly came from Orwell reading Huxley's Brave New World and thinking that the emphasis on pleasure and distractions was entirely wrong. If I recall, this is why in his book the Victory Gin tasted so bad and the cigarettes were of such poor quality. Rather than Huxley's "feelies" and constant promiscuity, sex in 1984 is described almost an act of defiance.

As of now, I think Huxley had the more accurate depiction.

replies.title: Re: The Apocalyptic Impulse
replies.content: Again, that's because Orwell was writing in Britain just after 1949. Did you know that wartime rationing continued there until the early 1950s? The gin really was bad (and "Victory Gin" was meant to recall the constant "V for Victory" propaganda coming from the Big Brother Corporation aka BBC), and the cigarettes were of dismal quality. Remember that Britain had its empire taken away from it, and had to get used to being a much smaller and poorer country! Tolkien's "The Scouring of the Shire" was inspired by the same experience...

replies.title: Re: The Apocalyptic Impulse
replies.content:
I'd also add, having seen this dynamic play out, sometimes people become convinced that some horrific outcome is inevitable, and try to bring it about in a way that will be "less bad". If, to use your example, blanket surveillance is inevitable, would you rather it be done by someone who is bound by laws, or someone who is not?

If it really is inevitable, then it really does make sense to try to be the first so you can guide things in a less destructive direction.

replies.title: Re: The Apocalyptic Impulse
replies.content: Except that it never works out that way. Once the system is in place, it gets taken out of the hands of the "less bad" brigade and taken to its logical extremes. A glance at the history of nuclear weapons is a good case study here, with Oppenheimer in the "less bad" category and Teller in the "as bad as it can get" category.

replies.title: Re: The Apocalyptic Impulse
replies.content:
A Wendell Berry quote might be apt here:
"The discipline of ends is no discipline at all. The end is preserved in the means; a desirable end may perish forever in the wrong means. Hope lives in the means, not the end."
From "Discipline and Hope" in A Continous Harmony

replies.title: Re: The Apocalyptic Impulse
replies.content:
I actually met Edward Teller once, in my 'teens. That very brief encounter left me thoroughly shaken.

I was schoolmates and friends with his son, Paul (who was one year/grade younger than I), and I went to his house once to visit him. Paul was out, but his father answered the door and told me Paul wasn't at home just then. That was the one and only time I even met the man. But those few seconds at the door have stayed with me ever since.

What struck me then was the man's intensity, for lack of a better word. He was the most intense person I had ever encountered up to then, and maybe ever since. He struck me as utterly consumed with boundless fury against the whole living world, a ferocity barely held in check by his iron self-control and very high intelligence. I came away from those few seconds with a new and somewhat frightening appreciation of the potential of humankind.

Something of the same sort seems to have been perceived by others in him. The University of California's memorial for him contains this striking characterization: "he was a force of nature as much as a human being." [senate.universityofcalifornia.edu/_files/inmemoriam/html/EdwardTeller.htm]


So ... "as bad as it can get" seems right on target to me.

replies.title: Re: The Apocalyptic Impulse
replies.content: I gotta ask out of sheer curiosity--what was his son like in terms of intensity/playfulness/creativity/intelligence/etc?

replies.title: Re: The Apocalyptic Impulse
replies.content: i don't know whether JMG will put this through so late, but I just now saw your question.  Paul was a humble and kind person.  I remember him as wearing sandals or flip-flops most of the time  IIRC, he was an advocate for world peace.  his father mentioned him only very, very briefly in his autobiography: "my gentle son Paul."  Paul's sister, Wendy, was mentioned more often in their father's book.  Paul became a professor of philosophy.  I haven't been in touch with him for more than half a century now.

comments.title: Regardie Says: Loosen Up!
comments.content:
I’ve been reading Regardie’s The Art of True Healing. I think it’s pure gold. There’s much in it that I would like to discuss. But for now I’ll address just one critical point. Regardie says:

“Physical inflexibility as well as mental and emotional tension… actually prevent one from becoming aware of the ever-presence of the life force… When the mind’s petty barriers are eliminated, and life flows through its extensive organization, we become conscious of the dynamic principle pervading and permeating all things.” (p. 62)

Due to personal history I have chronic tension and stiffness in the lower back, in the area said to be associated with the sacral chakra. It takes me about twenty to forty-five minutes of self-persuasion - light self-hypnosis I suppose - to eliminate much of this tension. Within twenty-fours or sooner it returns.

I don’t want to be hampered in magic - or prayer, as Regardie evidently views it - by chronic stiffness. I can’t afford regular massages. I know some yoga, but am afraid to do much because of a hip injury. Do you happen to know of any relatively easy, relatively brief practices that could help to release tension in such an area?

replies.title: Re: Regardie Says: Loosen Up!
replies.content: For the time being, do some whole body relaxation every day -- work on releasing excess tension from all over your body. Even if you can only do five minutes a day, that will help considerably over time.

comments.title: Druid Grove Formation
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid John,

I have been tasked to set up a druid grove in the Far East. Since most of the potential members are of the Christo-Druid variety, I would like to place the grove under the patronage of St Lazarus of Bethany (JN 11:1-44). However, we are open to non-Christian druids.

Some of the potential members are also Christo-Wiccans. A question has come up. Will the egregores of the Christo-Wiccans blend in with those of the Christo-Druids, or will both organizations have separate egregores? Will the non-Christian druids' egregore also blend in? We aim to be inclusive of all varieties of druids.

Do you have any practical advice for me? What practices from your books would you recommend? I am considering introducing MOE material to the members in stages, as healing will be part of the grove's mission. This is another reason for adopting the patronage of St Lazarus. What working do you recommend to reach out to St Lazarus? I am familiar with FHR and MOE materials, as well as some GD.

replies.title: Re: Druid Grove Formation
replies.content:
I've never worked with "Christo-Wiccans" -- I take it that's some kind of fusion of Wicca and Christianity -- so there I can't help you. As for the others, if they're all willing to be tolerant of each other's faiths and patron deities, it should work out just fine. With regard to practices, focus on the grove opening and closing and the first grade of initiation to begin with -- that'll feed the egregor and make the rest go smoothly.

As for workings with saints, I don't do those, so you'll probably need to talk to a more experienced Christian to find that out.

replies.title: Re: Potpourri, Political Astrology
replies.content:
1) Delighted to hear it! The drops Jenny used were an essential oil blend -- I'm not sure of everything that's in it, but attar of roses, essential oil of frankincense, and a little cinnamon leaf oil are included. If you're interested in creating a blend for your own altar, I recommend picking up a few books on aromatherapy -- your local public library will have some -- and choosing the oils on the basis of what you find there.

2) That's something that happens with any good study course. After you finish this pass through the books, put them away for a few years and then read through them again; you'll be astonished by what you find.

See how those charts relate to his birth chart. Also, if you know how to progress a natal chart, progress his chart to the year in which the election takes place, and check the progressed chart against the natal chart and the chart for the election itself.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I'm starting to wonder if the entire internet was doomed from the start for a magical reason, and am curious if you think this analysis makes sense. The internet traces its roots back to ARPAnet, which was created to solve a single and simple problem: the problem of unused computing. At the time, computers were massive machines, cost insane amounts of money, and at any given moment some of them were idle while others had backlogs of people trying to use them. The entire idea of computer networking was invented to solve this problem, by allowing people to use other computers than the one located where they are.

It took years of work and experiments by a very large number of phenomenally bright minds, who also spent quite a long time trying to figure out how to ensure it could be scaled up beyond the initial very small network. I'm unaware of any formal magical workings, but given there was quite a bit of overlap between the computer science and occult spheres, it seems plausible one happened quietly. Even if no formal workings happened having a large number of very focused minds obsess over this topic for years as they tried to make it work would probably have given the nascent internet an egregor pushing it in the direction of trying to keep computers being used as much as possible.

The core problem here is that it almost always makes sense to try to have more computing capacity than you expect to use at the time of lowest demand; otherwise, it is impossible to meet the highest demands. This means there will almost always be at least some spare capacity, and so a drive for finding a use for all computers at all times would end up driving a lot of useless things.

Once it expanded far enough, the next logical step would be to link every computer in the world and shift things which were long done without them online; then to create a legion of spambots; and so on, until you reach the point where much of the internet's traffic is robots trying to trick other robots into thinking they are humans, another large fraction is robots scouring the internet to build these robots, and another large piece of it is composes of robots trying to scam people, and other robots trying to waste these robot's time.

From any kind of sane perspective, none of this makes sense, and a lot of very bad ideas had to come together in just the right way for us to get here. However, if the goal is to avoid unused computing power, then it is hard to think of a better system than the one we currently have.

Do you think this sort of analysis makes sense?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It makes quite a bit of sense. Do you recall the story of the sorcerer's apprentice, and the mops and brooms that get out of control and keep on doing their job in ever-increasing and ever more destructive fashion? It's not impossible that, whether explicit magic was involved or not, that's what we have now.

The octagram represents a two way relationship between two equals, where each needs to respect the freedom of the other. At its center is an octagon, which is the shape of many road signs meant to get drivers to be more mindful of each other, notably the stop sign, which is there to make sure the freedom of two drivers to cross an intersection will not lead to an accident. replies.title: Re: Thanks and Graditude for the Golden Scetion Fellowship
replies.content:
OP here, I looked it up. According to Wikipedia, the honor goes to Detroit police sergeant Harold "Harry" Jackson, who was tasked with traffic duty at an intersection where visibility was low on one side. He wanted drivers to slow down, so he took a square sign, wrote the word "stop" on it, and cut off the corners, apparently to increase visibility and make the sign more distinctive. He then saw this had the effect of drivers slowing down and being more careful around the intersection.

So, no, it appears the inventor of the sign was not aware of the symbolic significance of the octagon, but I find it interesting the sign seemed to have influenced driver's behavior before being official or mandated by law.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Greetings JMG!

How does Eliphas Levi's explanation of the primary and secondary relate to Platos notion of the cave of shadows? Are they both a similar idea of the primary or 'ideal' creating an reflected secondary or have I got this completely wrong?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Plato's cave is considerably more complex. Remember that the shadows in the cave aren't being projected by the primary light -- they're artificial shadows, manufactured by the demiurge. (The Republic is to my mind the most important inspiration for early Gnosticism.) So in Plato you have four levels: the primary light; its reflections, which can only be seen once you're outside the cave and your eyes get used to the light; the fire of the demiurge; and the artificial shadows cast by that fire, which people treat as realities.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What would be an example in life of the fire of the demiurge?   False projections of society? Thank you…

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's certainly one way of thinking about it. Keep in mind that Plato created his narratives to have many meanings, which can be unpacked in meditation.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Or you could look at the allegory of the cave as a map, covering the same territory as Qabala.
In John Gilbert's version of it, the primary light would correspond to the source of everything that exists in Kether, and the world of reflections corresponding to the World of Air or the World of Archetypes. Then the fire of the Demiurge would be the World of Water, a reflection of the Archetypal World across the Abyss (or the mouth of the cave), and the World of Fire, or the World of Illusions would be the shadows on the wall. The wall itself is the material universe or the World of Earth. Thus, the fire of the Demiurge is the place where the emotions are born, which then influence our perceptions of the world, which belong to the World of Fire, or the shadows in Plato's allegory.

Good Morning Archdruid,

I’ll go straight to the point. My mother passed away a month ago. We are three daughters, but honestly, I was the only one by her side – not just in her final weeks, but caring for her for years from 4800 miles away- as if I were an only child. When she passed, I did wonder: “Where were they?” Not for me, but for her. I gave her my 110%, but one child can’t replace the other two.

Just to give you some context: we’re from Argentina, a very family-oriented Latin culture.

After her passing, my sisters completely lost it. They turned into greedy people I don’t even recognize. My sense is they’re trying to fill, with “stuff” (there isn’t much anyway), the hole they feel with Mom gone. "You can have it all, I have something you will never have" are some of the maybe "unfortunate" phrases that I told them during arguments. Not ideal, but I deeply feel that. I think they carry deep guilt – especially my sister who is a doctor. During Mom’s 6-7 weeks of hospitalization, she took a 3-week vacation with her friends. They only showed up for the last five days when morphine was already running through Mom’s veins.

Whatever is going on with them is their issue to address with their therapists or souls. But they’ve made me the target of their unresolved feelings and regrets, and I feel bombarded by their hatred and horrible energy. Even though I am at peace with my choices – being by my mother’s side and ensuring her peaceful passing – their behavior is affecting my sleep. I’m having terrible nightmares. So while I tell myself it’s “their problem,” it’s clearly impacting me more than I admit.

I immediately distanced myself, moving to my aunt’s house before returning to the US, and since then have had no contact except when they “need” something, as I stepped down from handling Mom’s affairs.

This comes from a former nine-year Catholic nun, so you know the tool belt I count on.

I hope you can give me some guidance of how to shield and protect myself from so much hatred.

Apologies for such a lengthy post and thank you for your time,

Maria

replies.title: Re: Family Conflict / Hatred Shield
replies.content: It's really sad that people so often lose it so completely when dealing with the loss of someone close to them. I would encourage you to take up the St. Michael prayer daily to protect yourself against the noxious energies your sisters are letting loose; I would also encourage you to pray the rosary daily, if you don't do so already, asking the Virgin to bring peace to your family.

comments.title: A long dream
comments.content:
Happy Monday JMG & all,

A handful of years ago was a time in my life in which I kept a serious dream journal and was experiencing many vivid, at times lucid dreams.

One of these dreams involved me living in an island shack with someone who I have never (to my memory) met in waking life.

This was a very vivid dream in which I was in control of my actions and thoughts but was not aware that I was dreaming.

What sets this dream apart from others and brings me to my question today is the length of the dream. To my dreaming-self, the dream last for over a week of full days and full activities, continuously.

The woman who I was living with, Earnie (spelled that way in the dream) and I were long term romantic partners within the dream’s context. I went to a barbecue with her family, looked through scrapbooks, and lived a normal day to day life on this island with her.

At one point I remember having the thought of “how can I tell Earnie I’ll be leaving soon?”

The dream ended by falling apart. I remember rising water, chaos, and someone who seemed to be Kanye West painted completely blue aggressively asking me for advice.

Such a real-feeling dream, and I woke up feeling completely unreal, a feeling like “whoa” — like having to actively settle back into this waking life.

I still think about Earnie sometimes, and miss her in the way you would miss an old friend whose address you’ve lost.

In sharing all of this, I suppose my question is: what are other peoples’ experience with such lengthy dreams?

comments.title: Symbolism and combustion
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I have two questions about the sky today, thank you so much for considering them:

1. I had an argument about the symbolism of sun and moon with the moon being associated with feminity. Have you read anything about the "ancients" (however defined) already knowing that the moon reflects the light of the sun (receiving vs. radiating)? Or does the association come about because the length of the moon's cycle is similar to the female one?

2. I know you're not a natal astrologer but this is rather about the literature: do you know a book of early 20th-century astrology that talks about "combust" and "cazimi". Or is that just in classical astrology? My natal Saturn is combust, my wife's Mars even cazimi (8') and I'm wondering how that's different than a regular conjunction. My edition of Llewellyn's "A to Z Horoscope Make and Delineator" from 1974 doesn't mention those terms at all, however, and I'm wondering if other books from that era do.

replies.title: Re: Symbolism and combustion
replies.content:
1) To begin with, it simply isn't true that the Moon is consistently associated with the feminine. In Japan and among the old Nordic peoples, the Sun is female and the Moon is male; in some Northwest Coast Native American nations, the Sun and Moon are male twins; while in Egypt and Mesopotamia alike, the Moon was a god -- Thoth/Djehuti in the one, Sin in the other. As for the ancients and lunar light being reflected, you might glance at the Orphic Hymn to the Moon: "Female and male with borrowed rays you shine..." Any observational astronomer will quickly discover that the lit side of the Moon always faces the Sun, and many cultures drew the logical conclusion.

2) Combustion is in George's book -- you'll find it referenced on p. 709. Ivy Goldstein-Jacobson discusses it in a couple of her books, and it's all through the horary literature of the time, since that was almost entirely based on Lilly. Cazimi isn't something I've found in that literature at all, but then I've only ever heard of it in books on astrological magic.

replies.title: Re: Symbolism and combustion
replies.content: In Russian and some other Slavic languages one of the two usual words for the moon (месяц) is masculine in its grammatical gender; the other (луна) is feminine  The word for the sun (солнце) is neuter.

replies.title: Re: Symbolism and combustion
replies.content: Hmm! And the feminine one is an import from Latin, while the masculine one looks to me like the product of an archaic Indo-European root.

replies.title: Re: Symbolism and combustion
replies.content: Yes indeed (about луна < luna)!

replies.title: Re: Symbolism and combustion
replies.content: An interesting ancient source to look at about the moon is Plutarch's "On the Face in the Moon".

I realize this question isn't all that important in the grand scheme of things--just a curiosity on my part.

replies.title: Re: Long-term Karma of inadvertent actions
replies.content:
I think one of the reasons the ancients used to credit such inventions to the gods is to avoid this kind of misunderstanding. To begin with, no one person invented any of these things -- the emergence of every kind of writing was a slow process that started out with crude little sketches of physical things (six ox heads representing six oxen) and morphed bit by bit from there to phonetic writing; the printing press was invented over a period of half a millennium, starting in early medieval China; and the first experiments that led to our current use of electricity took place in ancient Greece.

Second, the people who participated in inventing writing, or any of these other things, only had a karmic legacy for what they themselves did with their contribution. For example, the smart but utterly forgotten Phoenician who first started using sloppily drawn Egyptian hieroglyphs to represent individual sounds, and so created the first very rough draft of an alphabet, bears no karmic responsibility for my books -- that's all on me.

replies.title: Re: Long-term Karma of inadvertent actions
replies.content:
1) Thanks!
2) I should have specified that I realize that no one person invented any of this--I more meant these as illustrative as a concept.

However, part of your answer threw me for a bit of a loop in that I thought that the myths of what happened to Prometheus ("inventor", sort of, of fire) and Daedalus (inventor of labyrinth, Icarus' wings, automaton, etc.) and the likes was meant to symbolize that the inventor can't escape (no pun intended wrt labyrinth) the consequences of his design. If that was not the intention, what is your read on this, just out of curiosity.


Also, does it make me a bad person to wish that those people who came up with those internet popup ads have to suffer through an infinite number of them for all eternity? :)

replies.title: Re: Long-term Karma of inadvertent actions
replies.content:
Prometheus got into trouble not by inventing but by repeatedly annoying the gods. As for Daedalus, his story is a great parable about the downsides of genius.

My approach to internet pop-up ads is far more cruel. I use a browser with a good ad blocker, so from my perspective, the people who create pop-up ads have wasted their entire lives, since I never see their work!

replies.title: Re: Long-term Karma of inadvertent actions
replies.content:

If I want to become a brilliant inventor, what kinds of things could I do to aid in that?

replies.title: Re: Long-term Karma of inadvertent actions
replies.content: I recommend doing the same thing that Mozart did to become a brilliant musician -- spend the next half dozen lives inventing things, so you develop a talent for it and bring that into each successive life. I'm pretty sure Tesla was a product of the same process.

replies.content:

I mean, I guess he might have, but at least historically-speaking, he didn't become as well known--Bach preceded Mozart and Beethoven was more or less a contemporary.

replies.title: Re: Long-term Karma of inadvertent actions
replies.content: Yes, I think there's a good case to be made that the soul of Antonio Vivaldi reincarnated as Mozart. As for why he didn't just keep on reincarnating -- well, one of the points of material incarnation is to outgrow the need for material incarnation; being in bodies of matter is more or less the prenatal state of the soul. With any luck Mozart wrapped up his remaining karma in his short life and went on to better things. Since all things in the world of matter are clumsy and imperfect reflections of realities on other planes, he's doubtless had plenty to do.

replies.title: Re: Long-term Karma of inadvertent actions
replies.content:

comments.title: The Complications of Being Generous and a Question about Unlucky People
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I have two questions this week.

First, in one of your comments here on Magic Monday, you mentioned that if between lives, one would like to have a more “heavenly” experience during their life review, they should be loving and generous when they are alive. I have taken this advice to heart, and first, reflected on how I have been a bit stingy at these behaviors, and second decided that I should start being more generous and loving.

Perhaps it was the wrong place to try out such positive thoughts and deeds, but I decided to give it a try on a long cross country car trip I recently made where I was the driver. If another driver behaved in a rude manner towards me, I consciously challenged my normal habitual negative reaction towards them, into giving them the benefit of a doubt, and tried not to take their behavior personally especially when I could see that they were tailgating or cutting off other drivers as well. But then I hit a grey area in my generosity and loving thoughts. Several times a particular pattern began to happen where I could no longer be generous towards certain drivers. These were drivers who decided to risk both our lives. Several times when I was driving in the left lane, about to catch up with a truck driving in the right lane (so as to pass the slower truck) a driver from the right lane would zoom up to me in the right lane and then cut in front of me to pass the truck before I could. I had several close calls where the other drivers almost hit me while squeezing between my hood and the back of the truck, in order to pass in front of me. Suffice to say, I found it difficult to be loving toward those drivers. So my first question is, how can one be generous and loving when there are people out there who take advantage of generous people?

Second, I have a friend who seems to be bad luck to be around. She is a well-meaning person, and smart, but is plagued by making poor choices and is severely lacking in common sense. It’s almost funny in how when she decides to do something or buy something it inevitably turns out to be a bad idea. And when I am with her she peer pressures me into making bad choices, too, or I fail at things I can normally do well when she’s not around.

I have another friend who habitually makes bad choices, too. He is a chronic complainer, and all his problems are someone else’s fault, he says. He stayed with me for a while when he became homeless and his negativity was so intense that I literally ended up in the hospital from a heart complication. When I was released from the hospital I had to throw him out of the house, just so I could survive.

So my second question is, can someone else’s bad energy rub off on you? Does karma have something to do with this?

replies.title: Re: The Complications of Being Generous and a Question about Unlucky People
replies.content:
1) The opposite of one bad idea is another bad idea; being too generous, and thus being exploited, is just as unwise as being too stingy. The kind and loving thing to do for all concerned is sometimes to limit your generosity to those who earn it.

2) Yes, this happens, because human consciousness isn't locked up inside individual skulls. Sometimes it's necessary to do as you did, and distance yourself from those who go out of their way to generate negativity and suffering. Sometimes, as the samurai used to teach, unconditional love is best expressed with the sharp edge of the sword.
